articles.title: The Dust of the Country Road
articles.author: Annie Pike Greenwood
articles.complete_text:
The dust of the country road lies quiet and dull.
As I watch a spot stir, it waves and rises and whirls;
Down the street it goes whirling in ecstatic freedom,
Goes whirling and sinking and rising,
It is the dust of one who has lain through the year,
Yearning and dreaming until it has passioned the strength to be 'up and away.

I, too, am dust,
I, too, yearn and dream,
O happy dust, when shall I rise and away?
Living dust, nothing can rise of itself,
I lay here praying, expectant, awaiting
'Til the arms of God, thru His breezes,
Lifted me up and bade me begone
articles.notes: This is a complete poem by Annie Pike Greenwood that appears at the end of the misc content, separate from the table of contents and advertisements. It's a standalone literary work that should be preserved as its own file.

misc_summary: The remaining content consists of legitimate misc material: table of contents, publication information, advertisements for various products and services (Carnation Milk recipe, Temple clothes, flowers, garments, children's clothing), Red Cross conference announcement, and general business/subscription information.
